When we say like, oh, YouTube's algorithm didn't like it, or anytime you try to blame something on YouTube's algorithm, replace the word algorithm with...
Audience, right?
Because all YouTube's algorithm is trying to do is optimize for the audience.
And what YouTube wants more than anything is watch time.
So when you look at your video, there are really only two components that make up watch time.
What's the click-through rate and what's the retention?
And if you have good click-through rate and you have good retention, you're going to have a ton of views because YouTube is going to show it to a bunch of people.
But it does get confusing because as your channel starts to grow, you get a subscriber base.
And then you might publish a video that has a really high retention and click-through rate but only amongst your subscribers.
And as soon as YouTube tried to show that video to someone who didn't know who you were or what your channel was, the click-through rate retention plummeted.
So then you might see a video with amazing click-through rate, amazing retention, but it has crappy views because no one outside of the people who know your channel were watching it, right?
Yeah, that's just where it's...

And that is going to make it, I think, quite difficult to find an audience because, okay, so the way that YouTube, my understanding of the way YouTube's algorithm works is it works based on tranches.
Which is a very fancy French word that I absolutely love.
So, whoever.
Whoever watches your channel the most, when you publish a new video, YouTube is going to show that video to the people who watch your content the most.
And if they watch it, if they click on it, if they have good retention, then it's going to go out to the next tranche.
People who maybe have seen you a couple of times, but not super familiar with it, if they engage with it, watch it, retain, then it goes to the next tranche, and so on and so on and so on, right?

So the challenge, if your topic is all over the place, when you publish a new video, YouTube shows it to that first tranche, and like, well, you had 500 people watch a video about writing content, and now you're publishing a video about building a SaaS.
Well, somebody who's interested in writing content probably isn't interested in building a SaaS, so then the SaaS video fails that first tranche, and YouTube doesn't show it to anyone else.
So that's another reason why, you know, and you see this so often, like if you look at bigger creators, they'll have like two, three, four, five different channels.
Because if they publish something different on their main channel, it flops like crazy because it fails that first tranche test.
